chunk_summary: Deworming in SAM management is a rehabilitation-phase intervention, initiated after clinical stabilization, with age-specific anthelmintic dosing and hygiene counseling to prevent reinfection.
learning_objectives: Understand the timing and rationale for deworming in SAM rehabilitation.
Learn age-specific anthelmintic dosing protocols for albendazole and mebendazole.
Recognize contraindications and adverse effects of deworming in unstable children.
Integrate deworming with hygiene counseling and outpatient follow-up workflows.
key_facts: Deworming is not a day-0 priority in SAM management; it is initiated after 48–72 hours or at discharge.
Albendazole dosing: 200 mg single dose for 12–23 months, 400 mg single dose for ≥24 months.
Mebendazole alternative: 100 mg twice daily for 3 days in children ≥24 months.
Avoid deworming in children <12 months unless directed by specific program policies.
Persistent diarrhea requires stool examination for pathogens like giardiasis, not empiric deworming.
Link deworming to hygiene counseling (handwashing, feces disposal, footwear) to reduce reinfection.
reference_quote: Deworming is a rehabilitation-phase intervention in SAM, not a day-0 priority, because early inpatient care should focus on hypoglycemia, hypothermia, infection treatment, and careful feeding. (Source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Operational guidelines on facility-based management of children with severe acute malnutrition, 2011)
document_key_topics: Contraindications and adverse effects of deworming
